coredata.dc:title: Multi-responsive graphene quantum dots hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel for information encoding and encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Jingye
Pan, Guoyi
Lin, Wenjing
Tang, Zilun
Zhang, Jieheng
Li, Jiaxin
Li, Wenping
Lin, Xiaofeng
Luo, Hongsheng
Yi, Guobin
coredata.dc:description: Information security plays an important role in the era of big data. Attempts to improve security often require giving them more additional encryption strategies. Herein, a specially designed multi-responsive graphene quantum dots hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel system was proposed. Firstly, monomers such as N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM), and dimethylamino ethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) were filled into the photonic crystal templates and then photo-cured. After hydrofluoric (HF) etching, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the precursor solution containing dithiothreitol (DTT) and borax were filled into the inverse opal holes. Finally, the hybrid hydrogel integrating quantum dots and photonic crystals was prepared by curing at room temperature. The hybrid hydrogel exhibited excellent pH, temperature, and angle-responsive color-changing characteristics. Besides, it had fluorescent properties under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation and had photothermal conversion ability under near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation. Furthermore, the hybrid hydrogel color matrix was prepared by utilizing its excellent quantum dots fluorescent properties, dynamically adjustable structural color, multi-responsive, and self-healing properties. Therefore, the color matrix formed by the hybrid hydrogel had strong information storage capacity and encoding capacity, which could perform various information encryption and decryption modes step-by-step. Furthermore, we developed a simple mini-program for the step-by-step identification and decryption process of the matrix, indicating that it had great practical value in the field of information security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-responsive
Structural color
Self-healing
Hybrid hydrogel matrix
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of silica photonic crystal templates
Preparation of PHEMA-PNIPAM-PDMAEMA inverse opal hydrogel
Preparation of GQDs hybrid multi-responsive structural color self-healing hydrogel
Rational construction of GQDs hybrid multi-responsive structural color self-healing hydrogel
Preparation and optical properties of HND inverse opal hydrogel
Characterization of HND inverse opal hydrogel and its multi-responsive properties
Characterization and properties of GQDs/borate hydrogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (a) fabrication and (b) information encryption and decryption of multi-responsive GQDs hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel.

Preparation of HND inverse opal hydrogel and its optical properties. (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of HND inverse opal hydrogel. (b) SiO2 photonic crystal templates with different colors. (c) SEM images of SiO2 photonic crystal templates, SiO2-HND hydrogel, HND inverse opal hydrogel (left to right) and their (d) reflection spectra. The scale bars in (c) are 500 nm, 500 nm, and 2 μm (left to right).
HND inverse opal hydrogel under different pH and temperature conditions: (a) photographs of “bear”; (b), (c), (d), and (e) reflection spectra. The scale bar in (a) is 0.5 cm.
HND inverse opal hydrogel under different pH and angel conditions: (a) photographs of “bear”; (b), (c), (d), (e) reflection spectra. The scale bar in (a) is 0.5 cm.

Properties of GQDs/borate hydrogel. (a) Fluorescence photographs of GQDs solution. (b) FT-IR spectra of GQDs/borate hydrogel. (c) Photographs of GQDs/borate hydrogel combination pattern. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs solution and GQDs/borate hydrogel.

Preparation of GHND and its properties. (a) Preparation and self-healing process of GHND. (b) SEM image, (c) reflection spectra, (d) self-healing performance, (e) photographs and (f) reflection spectra of the “bear” under NIR irradiation. (g) The relationship between GHND temperature change and NIR irradiation time at different GQDs solution concentrations. The scale bars in (b), (d), and (e) are 2 μm, 1 cm, and 0.5 cm, respectively.

Preparation of anti-counterfeiting label and its application. (a) Encoding terms of matrices transformation in anti-counterfeiting label. (b) Information storage process of the anti-counterfeiting label. (c) A conceptual application of the anti-counterfeiting label.

head.ce:title: Multi-responsive graphene quantum dots hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel for information encoding and encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jingye Yan and Guoyi Pan contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Information security plays an important role in the era of big data. Attempts to improve security often require giving them more additional encryption strategies. Herein, a specially designed multi-responsive graphene quantum dots hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel system was proposed. Firstly, monomers such as N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM), and dimethylamino ethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) were filled into the photonic crystal templates and then photo-cured. After hydrofluoric (HF) etching, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the precursor solution containing dithiothreitol (DTT) and borax were filled into the inverse opal holes. Finally, the hybrid hydrogel integrating quantum dots and photonic crystals was prepared by curing at room temperature. The hybrid hydrogel exhibited excellent pH, temperature, and angle-responsive color-changing characteristics. Besides, it had fluorescent properties under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation and had photothermal conversion ability under near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation. Furthermore, the hybrid hydrogel color matrix was prepared by utilizing its excellent quantum dots fluorescent properties, dynamically adjustable structural color, multi-responsive, and self-healing properties. Therefore, the color matrix formed by the hybrid hydrogel had strong information storage capacity and encoding capacity, which could perform various information encryption and decryption modes step-by-step. Furthermore, we developed a simple mini-program for the step-by-step identification and decryption process of the matrix, indicating that it had great practical value in the field of information security.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security has attracted widespread attention since ancient times [1–4]. Methods of information protection have been gradually developed, from sealing wax and invisible ink to steganography, coding, etc.. Information coding is a strategy to store and transmit information by converting information into codes after reasonable design, which has been widely applied in the fields of communication and anti-counterfeiting [5–9]. Among various information encoding strategies, photonic crystals composed of periodically arranged nanoparticles have attracted so much attention due to their stable optical properties, and are thus widely used in the field of anti-counterfeiting [10–17]. However, single photonic crystal materials have inherent defects such as high rigidity, weak conductivity, and weak interactions between adjacent nanoparticles.

Smart hydrogels are an important subclass of “smart” materials, which are three-dimensional network structure polymer materials with excellent hydrophilicity, flexibility, stimulus responsiveness, etc. They show the rapid sol-gel phase or volume transition under external stimuli (such as pH, temperature (T), light, solvent, electric field, etc.), showing good application prospects in the fields of optical information security, tissue engineering, soft robotics, sensing, electronic skin, etc. [18–26]. Introducing smart hydrogel precursor into photonic crystals to prepare smart responsive photonic crystal hydrogel can make up for the deficiencies of single photonic crystal materials and is of great significance in the field of optical anti-counterfeiting [27–32]. As a representative work, inspired by the color-change of chameleon skin, Wu et al. constructed a non-closely packed photonic crystal structure based on zinc sulfide microspheres and temperature-responsive polymers by using a “two-step filling strategy”, showing dynamically adjustable structural color [33]. However, the current smart-responsive photonic crystal hydrogels for anti-counterfeiting only include one/dual stimuli-responsiveness and lack of multiple stimuli-responsiveness.

Moreover, these photonic crystal hydrogels could be damaged during the accumulated use process. Therefore, it is necessary to develop long service life photonic crystal hydrogel materials. Inspired by nature, self-healing hydrogels could complete the self-healing when they were damaged and improve the durability of hydrogel materials [34–36]. Taking advantage of the self-healing properties of hydrogels, it is possible to combine hydrogels with stimuli-responsive effects or to form composite materials composed of stimuli-responsive hydrogels and photonic crystal materials, which have been used in the fields of pattern display, information encoding, and encryption [37–43]. For example, Zhao et al. prepared structural color hydrogel with magnetic- and photothermal-controlled self-healing abilities. When the hydrogel got damaged, magnetic nanoparticles could absorb heat under NIR irradiation and external magnetic fields, which stimulated phase transformation in the hydrogel to heal the hydrogel, and thus the hydrogel was used for pattern display [44]. Wei et al. prepared two kinds of light-sensitive self-healing hydrogels and the hydrogels with different light-sensitive parts by programming the hydrogels. Under UV light irradiation, different parts had a different light-responsive effect, which was used for encoding and encryption of information [45]. However, few reports combine multiple stimuli-responsiveness with self-healing structural color hydrogels in the application of anti-counterfeiting.

This study was seeking an efficient material design that synergistically integrated the above features into a single multi-responsive self-healing structural color hydrogel system (Fig. 1 ). Herein, we adopted a two-step filling method to fill dual-responsive precursor (containing NIPAM and DMAEMA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) self-healing precursor (containing dithiothreitol (DTT) and borax) into the interstices and holes, and the stable multi-responsive self-healing structural color hydrogel was obtained, which integrated the properties of GQDs and photonic crystals (Fig. 1a). Based on these promising properties, we further explored robust GQDs hybrid multi-responsive structural color hydrogel with multi-responsive and dynamically adjustable color by individually changing the color of GQDs and photonic crystals or using different environmental stimuli (eg., pH, T, and light) to ensure their independent and continuous color combinations. In addition, we could explore the multi-encryption process of information and the idea of using a mobile phone mini-program for step-by-step decryption. Furthermore, the hydrogel material composed of dynamic borax bonds could self-healing, improving the durability of the material (Fig. 1b). Therefore, this GQDs hybrid multi-responsive self-healing structural color hydrogel has strong information storage capacity and coding capacity that can realize multiple information encryption and step-by-step decryption of mobile phone mini-program, which is expected to open a new chapter in the field of information security.

Anhydrous ethanol and sodium tetraborate were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ammonia and tetraethyl silicate (TEOS) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM), dimethylamino ethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA), hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA), N, N-methylene bisacrylamide (BIS), sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, hydrofluoric (HF), Dithiothreitol (DTT) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-1-phenyl-acetone (HMPP), polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA, M n = 700) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide and acetic acid were purchased from Guangzhou Reagent Co., Ltd., and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were purchased from Shanghai Sanmu Technology Co., Ltd. The water used in all experiments was purified using a water purification system (Shanghai Zhiang BEST-S30) with resistivity higher than 18 MΩ·cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silica photonic crystal templates
ce:section.ce:para: Silica (SiO2) nanoparticles with different particle sizes were synthesized by the modified Stöber method. 65 mL of anhydrous ethanol, ammonia, and 100 mL of pure water were added into a three-necked flask and stirred evenly at a high speed. Then, 185 mL of anhydrous ethanol and TEOS were poured into the above three-necked flask. Finally, after decreasing the magnetic stirring speed, SiO2 nanoparticles were prepared after 5 h at a constant temperature. By adjusting the amount of ammonia and TEOS, different particle sizes of SiO2 nanoparticles were prepared. The specific amount of ammonia and TEOS and stirring speed could be found in Table S1.

The SiO2 photonic crystal templates were prepared by the vertical deposition method. The SiO2 nanoparticles obtained in the above steps were dispersed in anhydrous ethanol to form the SiO2/ethanol dispersion liquid with a mass-to-volume ratio of 1.5 %. Then, the glass slide treated by the plasma cleaner was inserted vertically into the beaker containing the SiO2/ethanol dispersion, and the beaker was placed in a constant temperature and humidity chamber. The chamber temperature was set to 55 °C and the humidity was set to 65 % RH. After the ethanol was completely evaporated, SiO2 photonic crystal templates with different structural colors were prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PHEMA-PNIPAM-PDMAEMA inverse opal hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the template sacrificial method, PHEMA-PNIPAM-PDMAEMA (HND) inverse opal hydrogel was prepared. Firstly, 0.275 g NIPAM, 0.5 g HEMA, 0.01 g DMAEMA, 100 μL water, 20 μL HMPP as photoinitiator, 12 mg BIS as crosslinker were added into a beaker and mixed well to prepare HND precursor solution. Subsequently, 400 μL HND precursor solution was dropped on the surface of the SiO2 photonic crystal templates. And then the plexiglass plate with a thickness of 500 μm double-sided tape was used to cover the SiO2 templates to form a “sandwich” structure and photocured for 1 h under the irradiation of 36 W UV light. The cured SiO2-HND hydrogel was placed in the HF solution with a volume fraction of 4 %, and the SiO2 templates were removed by etching for 24 h to prepare the HND inverse opal hydrogel. Using the hollow pattern pressing technique, HND inverse opal hydrogel with different shapes could be prepared. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, acetic acid, and sodium hydroxide were used to prepare buffers solution with different pH to verify the pH responsiveness of HND inverse opal hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs hybrid multi-responsive structural color self-healing hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the GQDs powders were ultrasonically dissolved in pure water to prepare GQDs solutions with different concentrations. Then, 100 mg PEGDA (700) and 22 mg DTT were dissolved in GQDs solutions and then 0.1 mol/L borax solution was added. The mixture was stirred evenly to prepare GQDs/borate solution. The GQDs/borate solution could be solidified within 5 min at room temperature to prepare GQDs/borate hydrogel. Finally, before the GQDs/borate solution solidified, it was added dropwise to the surface of HND inverse opal hydrogel. The GQDs/borate solution would slowly penetrate the voids of the HND inverse opal structure framework. After solidification, the GQDs hybrid structural color hydrogel (GHND) was prepared.

The sample surface morphology investigation was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8220, Hitachi, Japan). The particle sizes of SiO2 were measured by a research-grade light scattering system (BI-200SM, Brookhaven, USA). FT-IR spectra were detected using a Fourier infrared spectrometer (IS50R, THEM OR-FISHER, USA). The reflection spectra of the samples were obtained by optical fiber spectrometer (Usb2000+, Ocean Optics, USA). A tensile machine (SUST, Zhuhai, China) with a stretching speed of 5 mm/min was used to measure the mechanical properties of the sample. The fluorescence emission spectra of the sample were measured by (FluoroMax-4, Halifax, France). The TEM images of three kinds of GQDs were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM, HT7700, Hitachi, Japan). The real-time thermal images were photographed by an infrared thermal imaging camera (MAG12-111703, Giant Electronics, China). The experimental optical imaging results were photographed by iphone12 mobile phone. The step-by-step identification and decryption process was completed by a self-developed simple mini-program.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational construction of GQDs hybrid multi-responsive structural color self-healing hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, an inverse opal hydrogel framework based on PHEMA-PNIPAM-PDMAEMA (HND) was prepared by a template sacrificial method, and then the framework was filled with GQDs/borate self-healing hydrogel solution to obtain the GQDs hybrid multi-responsive self-healing structural color hydrogel. Firstly, SiO2 nanoparticles with different particle sizes were synthesized by the modified Stöber method. By using the vertical deposition method, with the complete evaporation of the ethanol, SiO2 nanoparticles would self-assembled on the glass slide to form photonic crystal templates with a hexagonal close-packed structure. Then, the HND precursor solution was filled into the photonic crystal template to form a “sandwich” structure. The precursor solution would penetrate the interconnected nanopores between adjacent nanoparticles owing to the capillary force. Subsequently, SiO2-HND hydrogel was prepared under the irradiation of UV light. By further etching the SiO2 templates, the HND inverse opal hydrogel framework was obtained. Finally, to improve the durability and practical value of HND hydrogel, GQDs/borate hydrogel solution was filled into the HND inverse opal structure framework. Since the lateral size of GQDs is less than 50 nm, it could penetrate the framework voids along with the hydrogel filler. GQDs hybrid structural color hydrogel (GHND) was prepared after solidification at room temperature. HND inverse opal hydrogel, GQDs solution, and dynamic borax bonds endowed GHND with dynamically tunable structural color, fluorescence properties, photothermal conversion ability, and self-healing properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of HND inverse opal hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Using the template sacrificial method, the HND inverse opal hydrogel was prepared after HND hydrogel precursor filling into the SiO2 photonic crystal template and HF etching (Fig. 2 a and c). Since the SiO2 photonic crystal templates have a periodically arranged internal microstructure, it exhibit structural color visible to the naked eye macroscopically. We synthesized five different particle sizes of SiO2 nanoparticles and assembled them into photonic crystal templates by using the vertical deposition method. The particle sizes were 200 nm, 220 nm, 237 nm, 273 nm, and 284 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The structural colors of the corresponding photonic crystal templates were purple, blue, green, yellow, and red, respectively (Fig. 2b). The HND inverse opal hydrogel prepared by the template sacrificial method also had the same specific optical property - photonic band gap (PBG). Light of the same frequency located in the PBG is prevented from propagating and is completely reflected.

Therefore, we applied the Bragg's diffraction law to study the reflectance spectra data of SiO2 photonic crystal templates, SiO2-HND hydrogel and HND inverse opal hydrogel [46].
(1)
λ
max
=
2 d (
n
eff
2
-
sin
2
θ )
1 2
= 2.21 D
(2)
n
eff
2
= ∑
n
i
2
V i

The mathematical expression of Bragg's diffraction law is shown in formula (1), and the average refractive index of materials with periodic arrangement structure is shown in formula (2). Among them, λ max is the wavelength at the peak. d is the interplanar spacing of the face-centered cubic structure (111). θ is the angle between the incident light and the normal direction of the substrate. neff is the average refractive index. D is the diameter of the microsphere. When the natural light is vertically incident, θ = 0, d = (
6
3
) D.

It could be seen from the formula (1) and (2) that when the composition of the material was constant, λ max depended on d, which was related to the diameter of the colloidal crystal nanoparticles. Therefore, by adjusting the size of SiO2 nanoparticles, photonic crystal templates with different reflection peaks could be prepared, and the structural color could be widely tunable. When the HND precursor solution (nHND = 1.44) replaced the air (nair = 1) in the photonic crystal template voids, the reflection peak of the SiO2-HND hydrogel showed a trend of red-shifted due to the increase of the refractive index. However, due to the very close refractive index of the SiO2 and the HND precursor solution and the possibility of filling gap during the filling process, the full width at half maximum was broaden. Similarly, in the HND inverse opal hydrogel system, the buffer solution (nbuffer = 1.33) replaced the SiO2 (nSiO2 = 1.46), resulting in a trend of blue-shifted of the reflection peak due to the reduction of refractive index. In addition, the refractive index difference between the buffer solution and the HND precursor solution was larger than the refractive index difference between the SiO2 and the HND precursor solution, and the filling gap would disappear after HF etching, resulting in a narrowing of the full width at half maximum (Fig. 2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of HND inverse opal hydrogel and its multi-responsive properties
ce:section.ce:para: The HND inverse opal hydrogel was prepared by photo-initiated polymerization, and its chemical structure was showed in Fig. S2. A tensile machine with a stretching speed of 5 mm/min was used to measure the mechanical properties of the HND inverse opal hydrogel. The result showed that the it had certain mechanical properties with 102 % elongation at break (Fig. S3). Besides, FT-IR characterization was used to identify the successful preparation of HND hydrogel as well as HND inverse opal hydrogel. In the FT-IR spectra, the stretching vibration peak of the NH bond in NIPAM appeared at 3500–3250 cm−1. The asymmetric stretching of the CO bond and the bending vibration of the NH bond corresponded to the peak at 1644 cm−1 and 1543 cm−1 [47]. The stretching vibration of the OH bond in HEMA corresponded to the peak at 3289.83 cm−1. The stretching vibration of the CO bond corresponded to the peak at 1722 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the CO bond corresponded to the peak at 1150.80 cm−1 [48]. The characteristic peaks corresponding to DMAEMA were the stretching vibration of the CO bond at 1722 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the CN- bond at 1242 cm−1 [49]. The FT-IR spectra of HND hydrogel and HND inverse opal hydrogel almost overlapped, which indicated that the chemical structure of HND inverse opal hydrogel remained stable after HF etching (Fig. S4).

Thermodynamically, osmotic pressure is a partial derivative of Gibbs free energy change to the volume change, and total Gibbs free energy change (ΔG total) of the hydrogel is composed of the free energy of mixing between the hydrogel and the water (ΔG mix), the free energy arising from the difference in mobile ion concentrations inside and outside the hydrogel (ΔG ion) which is often described as Donnan potential, and the elastic restoring force (ΔG el) (formula (3)). When changing the hydrogel volume, the contribution from these three factors is balanced, and Π total becomes zero when the swelling equilibrium is reached. The mathematical descriptions for four of osmotic pressures are given in the Supporting Information [50–52].

To verify the temperature and pH responsive properties of the HND inverse opal hydrogel, we fixed the condition as θ = 0°. According to formula (3) to (6) in the Supporting Information, as the temperature increased, the Flory-Huggins interaction parameter χ decreased and Π mix increased due to the temperature-dependent phase behavior of NIPAM moieties in the polymer networks. And the volume of hydrogel decreased in order to make Π total become zero when the swelling equilibrium was reached, resulting in the decrease of the interplanar spacing d. And thus the structural color and the reflection peak of the HND inverse opal hydrogel showed a trend of blue-shifted based on formula (1). The corresponding reflection peak transitioned from 638 nm to 573 nm. Similarly, under other pH conditions, as the temperature increased from 25 °C to 50 °C, the structural color and the reflection peak of the HND inverse opal hydrogel showed a consistent trend (Fig. 3 a–e). Besides, it should be noted that the thermal-responsive property of the HND inverse opal hydrogel were reversible, as displayed from ten heating–cooling cycles (Fig. S5).

In addition, the presence of PDMAEMA induced a decrease in Π ion as the pH increased from 4 to 9 at T = 25 °C, which was mainly due to the deprotonation of amino groups in the hydrogel leading to the concentration differences inside and outside the hydrogel. Likewise, according to formula (3) to (6) in the Supporting Information, in order to make Π total become zero when the swelling equilibrium was reached, the volume of hydrogel decreased, resulting in the decrease of the interplanar spacing d. Then the structural color and the reflection peak of the HND inverse opal hydrogel showed a trend of blue-shifted based on formula (1). The corresponding reflection peak transitioned from 638 nm to 570 nm. Similarly, under other temperature conditions, the structural color and the reflection peak of the HND inverse opal hydrogel showed a consistent trend as the pH increased from 4 to 9 (Fig. 3a–e). Besides, the reflection spectra of HND inverse opal hydrogel before and after ten low pH-high pH cycles showed that the wavelength remained unchanged, revealing the stability and repeatability of HND inverse opal hydrogel (Fig. S6). Furthermore, we defined the reflection wavelength of HND inverse opal hydrogel at pH = 4, T = 25 °C and other conditions as λ0 , λ, respectively, and denoted approximately the swelling ratio of the hydrogel V/V 0 as λ/λ0 . As shown in Fig. S7, under constant pH conditions, the swelling ratio of the hydrogel decreased with increasing temperature. Similarly, under constant temperature conditions, the swelling ratio of the hydrogel decreased as the pH increased from 4 to 9. The above results further verified the temperature and pH responsiveness of the HND inverse opal gel.

In addition, due to the unique three-dimensional interconnected network structure of inverse opal HND hydrogel, its structural color is angularly dependent. In order to verify its angle dependence property, we fixed the environmental condition as T = 25 °C, pH = 4, and θ increasing from 0° to 45°. According to formula (1), the structural color and the reflection peak of the HND inverse opal hydrogel showed a blue-shifted trend. The corresponding reflection peak transitioned from 638 nm to 500 nm. Under other environmental conditions, the structural color and the reflection peak of the HND inverse opal hydrogel showed a consistent trend (Fig. 4 a–e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of GQDs/borate hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the principle of thiol-ene Michael addition reaction, we used borax, PEGDA, DTT, and GQDs solutions as raw materials to prepare GQDs/borate hydrogel by the one-pot method. Borax acted as a catalyst for thiol-ene Michael addition and formed a borate cross-linked network along with PEGDA and DTT segments. GQDs solutions have fluorescent properties due to their quantum size effect, and GQD-B, GQD-Y, and GQD-R emitted blue, yellow, and red fluorescence, respectively (Fig. 5 a). Their TEM images with around 15 nm particle size were showed in Fig. S8. Moreover, FT-IR analysis was performed to confirm that GQDs/borate hydrogel was prepared successfully. The stretching of the CC bond at 2549 cm−1 and the stretching of the SH bond at 1625 cm−1 almost disappeared after Michael addition reaction (Fig. 5b), indicating that the CC bond in PEGDA and the SH bond in DTT were almost completely reacted and the GQDs/borate hydrogel was successfully prepared [53]. Due to the existence of dynamic borax bonds, when the GQDs/borate hydrogel was damaged, the hydrogel would self-healing. To verify its self-healing ability, GQDs/borate precursor solution was poured into molds with different sizes to prepare blue fluorescent hydrogel (Gel-B), yellow fluorescent hydrogel (Gel-Y), and red fluorescent hydrogel (Gel-R) with different shapes and sizes. Based on the self-healing ability of GQDs/borate hydrogel, the above-mentioned hydrogel could be combined into a composite pattern with two fluorescent colors (Fig. 5c). In addition, two “stars” with the same shape and size but different fluorescence colors were divided into two parts and then put together to let them contact each surface at room temperature. It was found that these two parts of “stars” adhered to each other to form one “star” with two fluorescent colors, which further proved that the GQDs/borate hydrogel had great self-healing ability (Fig. S9). Besides, the fluorescence emission spectra showed that GQDs/borate hydrogel had the same fluorescence properties as GQDs solution (Fig. 5d).

To improve the durability and practical value of the HND inverse opal hydrogel, GQDs/borate self-healing hydrogel was filled into the HND inverse opal structure framework. The addition of GQDs/borate fillers endowed GHND with self-healing ability, fluorescence properties, and photothermal conversion ability (Fig. 6 a, b). After the GQDs/borate hydrogel was filled into the HND inverse opal framework, the reflection peak of GHND showed a trend of red-shifted owing to the increase of the refractive index (Fig. 6c). To verify the self-healing ability of GHND, the red and green rectangular GHND fragments were put together to let them contact each surface at room temperature. It was found that the above two fragments could be tightly adhered together, which was attributed to the existence of the dynamic borax bonds at the splicing point. When the GQDs/borate hydrogel was damaged, the dynamic borax bonds would rebind to obtain the complete GQDs/borate hydrogel (Fig. 6d). Besides, the reflection spectra test was performed on the splicing of GHND fragments. The result showed that two reflection peaks appeared corresponding to the reflection peak positions of the two GHND fragments, respectively (Fig. S10). Furthermore, due to the high photothermal conversion ability of GQDs, the as-prepared GHND was remotely controlled by absorbing the energy of NIR light. To explore the photothermal conversion ability of GHND, we prepared it into a “bear”. Under parallel NIR irradiation, the structural color of the “bear” showed a trend of blue-shifted. With the prolongation of the NIR irradiation time, the structural color of the “bear” exhibited a corresponding color transition from orange-yellow to green (Fig. 6e). Besides, the real-time thermal image of the “bear” showed that the temperature of the “bear” rose from 34.1 °C to 42.1 °C with the prolongation of the NIR irradiation time (Fig. S11). Since the structural color of the “bear” showed non-uniform changes, we selected the center of the “bear” as the reference point for the reflection spectra test for spectral tracking. Through increasing NIR irradiation time, the corresponding reflection peak transitioned from 595 nm to 495 nm, which was consistent with the changing trend of structural color (Fig. 6f). In addition, we also explored the effect of GQDs solution concentrations on the photothermal conversion ability of GHND. With the increase of the GQDs solution concentrations, the heating rate and the heating amplitude of GHND showed an increasing trend, indicating that the photothermal conversion ability of GHND increased as the increase of the GQDs solution concentrations (Fig. 6g). In summary, GHND had the advantage of fluorescence properties, photothermal conversion capability, self-healing properties, and dynamically tunable structural color, which showed that it had broad application in the fields of optical encoding and optical encryption.

In order to explore more secure encryption strategy, we further expanded the above properties. We cut the GHND into a square with a length and width of 1 cm (the thickness was uniform and could be ignored) by using a hollow punching tool. Each square had its unique structural color and fluorescence color. Based on the self-healing ability of GHND, the above squares were assembled into a matrix with a length of 4 cm and a width of 4 cm. Under the irradiation of visible light, a 4 × 4 cm structural color matrix was presented, and by adjusting the conditions (pH, T or θ), the structural color of the square could be changed to form a new color matrix. Under the irradiation of UV light, a 4 × 4 cm fluorescence color matrix was presented.

Combined with mobile phone identification technology, we prepared the above-mentioned matrices with structural color and fluorescence color under different conditions into anti-counterfeiting labels and applied them to the encryption and decryption of information. Firstly, we customized a set of encoding terms (Fig. 7 a). The six kinds of structural colors of purple, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red presented by squares in the color matrix were defined as Arabic numerals “1″, “2”, “3”, “4”, “5”, “6”, respectively. The blue, yellow, and red fluorescence colors presented by squares in the fluorescence matrix were defined as Arabic numerals “1”, “2”, “3”, respectively. Then, we converted the color matrices and the fluorescence matrices formed by the anti-counterfeiting labels under various conditions into digital matrices according to the encoding terms (Fig. 7b). Finally, the digital matrices were compiled into various information and stored in the mobile phone mini-program, and the information to be transmitted was hidden in the anti-counterfeiting labels.

As a conceptual example, under visible light irradiation, θ = 0°, T = 25 °C, pH = 9 conditions, the anti-counterfeiting label presented a color matrix A, which could be converted to a digital matrix
3
2
3
1
2
3
1
2
1
2
2
6
2
3
6
2
. When changing the conditions to θ = 0°, T = 25 °C, pH = 4, the anti-counterfeiting label presented a color matrix B, which could be converted into a digital matrix
4
3
5
2
3
5
2
3
2
3
3
6
3
5
6
3
. When further changing the conditions to θ = 0°, T = 35 °C, pH = 4, the anti-counterfeiting label presented a color matrix C, which could be converted into a digital matrix
3
2
4
1
2
4
1
2
1
2
2
6
2
4
6
2
. When further changing the conditions to θ = 30°, T = 35 °C, pH = 4, the anti-counterfeiting label presented a color matrix D, which could be converted to a digital matrix
3
2
3
1
2
3
1
2
1
1
2
5
2
3
5
1
. Besides, under the irradiation of UV light, the anti-counterfeiting label presented a fluorescent matrix E, which could be converted to a digital matrix
3
2
1
3
2
3
1
2
3
2
1
3
3
1
3
2
. Then the information receiver performed step-by-step identification and decryption according to the identification sequence (such as E → A → B → C → D) made by the encryptor (Fig. 7c). We further developed a simple mini-program for sequential identification of matrices to better demonstrate the step-by-step identification and decryption process (Video S1). Therefore, to obtain correct information, the information receiver must convert the above five digital matrices, and use a specific mobile phone mini-program to identify and decrypt them step-by-step. If the digital matrices or the recognition sequence were wrong, correct information could not be obtained.

The above information encryption and decryption system formed by the anti-counterfeiting label consist of five matrices. Each matrix consists of 16 small squares, and each small square may have six structural colors of purple, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red, as well as three fluorescence colors of blue, yellow, and red. Therefore, the information receiver must meet the following four conditions to decipher the hidden information of the anti-counterfeiting label: (1) obtain four color matrices and a fluorescence matrix through specific means; (2) convert four color matrices and a fluorescence matrix to digital matrices through custom encoding terms; (3) a specific mobile phone mini-program installed as a decoder for the identification of the digital matrices; (4) the digital matrices are recognized step-by-step according to a specific order. In the above four conditions, if any of the conditions are wrong, the correct information cannot be obtained. The encryption and decryption process involved the permutation and combination of four structural color matrices and one fluorescent color matrix. After mathematical permutation and combination calculation, the coding capacity of the encryption and decryption system was
16 6
4
×
16 3
5
=
16 135
, indicating that it had strong information storage capacity and encoding capacity. Besides, we could increase the number of small squares to increase the coding capacity of the anti-counterfeiting system. For example, we could design a n × n anti-counterfeiting system. After mathematical permutation and combination calculation, the encoding capacity of the above system was
n 12
4
×
n 6
5
=
n 270
. Therefore, the anti-counterfeiting labels can realize various information step-by-step encryption and decryption modes, and have great value in the field of information security.

This information encryption and decryption system have the potential to be applied to banking services, military secrets, cargo transportation services for insurance companies etc.. For example, insurance companies can use the above-mentioned anti-counterfeiting label to encrypt the safe for storing goods. The receiver needs to obtain the correct decryption methods and use the specific mobile phone mini-program as a decoder to open the safe to take out the goods. This multi-level encryption and decryption anti-counterfeiting label greatly improve the security of goods compared with the traditional safe that only uses a single digital password.

In conclusion, we developed multi-responsive GQDs hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel with comprehensive properties of broad multi-color tunability, multi-responsiveness, and self-healing ability. With excellent T/pH/angel response color-changing characteristics, and UV-irradiated fluorescence characteristics, the hybrid hydrogel was combined into color matrices. Then, the color matrices were converted into digital matrices to explore the potential applications of the step-by-step encryption and decryption process of information. These features indicate that the proposed multi-responsive GQDs hybrid self-healing structural color hydrogel matrices have strong information storage capacity and encoding capacity. Besides, we developed a simple mini-program for sequential identification of matrices to demonstrate the step-by-step identification and decryption process. Therefore, they are expected to open up new fields in the field of information security and have broad prospects in information encryption.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21808039, No. 51873042, and 51903058).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.138922.
